meta.title: ViFi — Contactless patient monitoring from $50 of WiFi hardware
meta.description: ViFi watches over hospital patients without touching them. Two small chips on the wall track heart rate and breathing, no wires, no wearables. Built to make continuous monitoring affordable for every hospital bed.

hero.tagline: ViFi watches over hospital patients without touching them. Two small chips mounted in the room track heart rate and breathing using only WiFi signals — no wires, no wearables, nothing for the patient to wear, charge, or remember. We're building this for the 80% of hospital beds where vitals are checked every 4 to 8 hours by a nurse, and missed in between.

stats.label: Patient effort
stats.sublabel: no wires, no wearables, no compliance

stats.label: Coverage
stats.sublabel: vs every 4–8 hours by a nurse

problem.heading: Most hospital patients are unmonitored more than 23 hours a day.
paragraphs: Outside the ICU — across roughly 700,000 U.S. hospital beds — a nurse takes vital signs every 4 to 8 hours. The rest of the time, no one is watching. Sepsis spikes, post-surgical complications, opioid-induced breathing problems: most of them happen in those gaps and only get noticed at the next round.

Continuous monitoring exists, but it costs about $3,000 per bed and means strapping wires and electrodes to people who often pull them off. So hospitals reserve it for the ICU. The much larger floor of ordinary patients gets nothing.
problem.pullquote: ViFi exists because the founder's mother spent 31 days getting diagnosed with Staphylococcus bacteremia after four separate visits, each time with vitals "stable" by the moment the nurse arrived.

how_it_works.heading: WiFi signals bend around bodies. We listen to how.
how_it_works.intro: Two small chips in the room exchange WiFi signals back and forth. Every breath you take and every heartbeat slightly changes how those signals travel through the air. Our software reads those changes as vital signs — no contact, no wearables, no line of sight required.
how_it_works.diagram:
[ESP32-S3 TX] ─────── 192 subcarriers ───────► [ESP32-S3 RX]
   antenna          (chest perturbs path)         antenna
                                                     │
                                                     │  USB serial @ 921600 baud
                                                     ▼
                                            csi_capture.py
                                                     │
                                                     ▼
                                       first_capture_report.py
                                                     │
                                                     ▼
                  HR / RR predictions  vs  Polar H10 ground truth
signal_path_steps: Parabolic peak refinement (cardiac and respiratory bands)
how_it_works.prior_art: The signal-processing approach is from peer-reviewed academic work — PhaseBeat (INFOCOM 2017), FullBreathe (UbiComp 2018), ResBeat (2020). ViFi's contribution is making it work on $20 commodity ESP32 chips instead of $500 research-grade WiFi cards, with the calibration, signal quality checks, and audit trail you need to go in front of an FDA reviewer.

paragraphs: Four paired captures, single subject, single room. Each capture pairs raw signal data from a 2-chip ESP32-S3 array with continuous heart rate from a Polar H10 chest strap as the ground-truth reference.
For each holdout we trained on the remaining sessions and scored the held-out one — a leave-one-session-out evaluation that estimates how the system generalizes to a session it has never seen.

Mean absolute error across the two scored holdouts: 3.89 and 4.41 beats per minute (mean 4.15). Per-window accuracy within ±5 bpm: 65–68%. PhaseBeat — the academic reference using $500 WiFi cards — reported 1.5 bpm. We did 4.15 on hardware that costs 20× less.
proof.results_link_text: Full methodology, captures, and per-session breakdowns: RESULTS.md
proof.placeholder_caption: [ photo of ESP32-S3 array + result chart goes here ]

roadmap.heading: The path to a hospital-approved medical device.
roadmap.intro: Today ViFi is a pre-clearance research prototype, not a medical device. From here, three steps stand between us and a contactless monitor running over a hospital bed:

steps.title: Prove it works for everyone, in any room
steps.body: Test ViFi on 10+ different people, in 3+ different rooms, against the same chest-strap reference. Goal: under 3 bpm error across subjects we've never seen.

steps.title: First hospital pilot
steps.body: Install ViFi in 5–10 hospital rooms alongside the existing standard of care. Compare what we see with what the hospital sees. Patient data stays on-site and de-identified; no clinical decisions are made from ViFi during the pilot.

steps.title: FDA approval
steps.body: Run a clinical validation study at an academic medical center, compile the safety and accuracy data, file with the FDA. This is what makes ViFi legally usable for medical decisions in U.S. hospitals.

hospitals.heading: We're recruiting pilot sites.
intro_paragraphs: We're talking to medical/surgical floor leaders, biomedical engineering teams, and clinical research offices interested in hosting a wellness-grade pilot of contactless monitoring.
pilot_bullets: 5–10 rooms instrumented with 2 ESP32-S3 boards each
30–90 day duration, IRB review where applicable
Runs alongside your existing monitoring; no clinical decisions are made from ViFi data
Output: a measurement study comparing ViFi against your existing reference

open.heading: The code is open.
open.body: ViFi is pre-incorporation and the engineering work lives publicly on GitHub — the signal pipeline, training scripts, prediction service, dashboard, calibration, fingerprinting, audit log, and 102-test suite. If you're a researcher, an engineer curious about WiFi-based sensing, a press contact, or just want to verify the 4.15 bpm number for yourself, read the code and the methodology.

footer.disclaimer_title: ViFi is a pre-incorporation research project.
footer.disclaimer_body: Not yet FDA-cleared. Research and development use only. The current system is not a medical device and must not be used for clinical decision-making.
